for four calls and then we added this one. So this is a; a bonus call.
You didn’t, you know, this is extra. You didn’t pay for this so if
it’s great, I’m sure you’ll love it and if we suck, remember you
didn’t pay for it and you can’t yell at us because; because it’s free.
But we won’t suck and Clayton never does. Now Clayton, before
we; I start to talk to you, you had reminded me, you said, “Bob,
last week you; you ended the call saying I should talk about”; I
was going to; I promised to talk about the two things that I learned
from you that I thought were most immediately useful to me. So
let me say what they are. No. 1, Clayton puts more of an emphasis
on building credibility very early than most other copyrighters I’ve
seen. And he does this; you look at the packages most of which
are tabloids and magalogs and you notice that most mag; magalogs
and tabloids you get just start with a headline, “Five Ways to Grow
Richer as Interest Rates Rise”. But Clayton’s pieces all have a;
what I call a; a credibility pre-head or some people call it an
eyebrow.
There’s a line of copy in smaller type above the headline; not a
subhead under it but there’s one above it and it’s always or almost
always dedicated to credibility. Like on this piece I’m writing; I’m
reading which Clayton I think you wrote this; For Wives, Five
Ways to Grow Richer as Interest Rates Rise.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah. 	It says at the top, “Martin D. Wise, Ph. D. who warned
that today’s rising interest rates 10 months in advance reveals dot,
dot, dot” and then it goes into Five Ways to Grow Richer as
Interest Rates Rise. So it’s to build credibility earlier than other
people do with a pre; with a pre-head. And the other interesting
idea that you gave when you; when you were talking about; I think
I’m under arrest here. Can anybody hear that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. (Laughter)
Bob Bly: 	I got to go guys. (Laughter) Flush that, will you?

off anything else that he’s ever done or that anyone in this industry
has done then your name’s going to get around very quickly and
your dance card’s going to be very full and you’re not going to
have to worry about raising your rates because your clients will do
it for you. As I said so many times, you don’t have to raise your
rates. All you have to do is write winners and then be unavailable
when somebody calls you to try to get on your dance card and they
will raise your rates for you.
So, on today’s call we’re going to be looking at copywriting
techniques. Some of them we’ve covered briefly before but we’re
going to try to get in more depth for you today. As far as theme;
selecting a theme is concerned it’s really an appropriate question
right now. I just returned from meetings in; in the Washington
D.C. area with a major client and we spent two days focusing on
two of that client’s newsletter franchises. And during those
meetings, we spent 100 percent of our time strictly talking about
what the product was. And I think this is a first; an important first
step whether you’re meeting with a client or whether you’re sitting
in your office at home and beginning the writing process. Step No.
1 really is what do I have to sell and taking a look at that that, in
this case it was a newsletter editor, a doctor and we talked about
who he is, how; how the impressive, physical impressive that he
gives, his training, his qualifications, his background and, and what
it is that he; he has a heart for in terms of what he wants to do for
his readers.
And by extension for his patients and we had to start with that. I; I
always say start with the prospect but this is really a preliminary
step. We didn’t write headlines. We didn’t list; we didn’t look at
the product itself. We just basically said you know what; what is
the positioning of this; of this particular doctor.
And again, please forgive me if; some of my examples are going to
be in areas that you’re not working in yet but I think if you think
about them, you will find application in the areas you; you are
working in. And we found this one doctor, for example, who is a

basically a walking testimonial for growing; for aging without

typically they’ll tell you its 60/40; 60 percent male 40 percent
female. But one thing that people don’t think about is a lot of
those guys on the file are really their spouses.
A promotion piece came to the; the household. The man may or
may not have subscribed but in many cases, it was addressed to the
man but the woman in the household ended up buying the product.
And so and that’s true on a lot of the lists that we’re going to
especially in the health area. So the mix is probably closer to
50/50 or maybe even it’s; it could be skewed toward the female

and how women think about themselves as they age.
And so I went through a long list of thoughts and oh course I’m 53
so I; it was very easy for me to write the male side of it. What do I
think about aging? What do I think about myself in the situation?
And these are just thoughts, beliefs and, and, and so forth about
this topic. Then I went in and I said how do I feel about each one
of those thoughts?
Remember we talked about the fact that thoughts trigger emotions
and so I asked myself how do I feel about each one of these
thoughts? Am I afraid? Am I disgusted? Am I frustrated? Am I
excited? You know what are my feelings?

What are they thinking about aging? And I found as I and, and I
wasn’t able to answer that question. I sat here and stared at my
computer screen and finally said what the heck am I trying to
answer this for? You know I don’t know anything about what it’s

And if Kim’s on the call, Kim, I think you were probably there. I
know some people from various publishers around D.C. were there
and, and so she had; she had both people in her prospect group but

minutes. (Laughter) And she just did a brain dump for me. It was

So I was able then to, instead of figuring it out on my own, I was
able to go ahead and, and address those issues of what a prospect is
thinking about the topic we’ve chosen, the general topic area and
how she must be feeling about; about this issue of growing older.
So that was step 1 and I call that my spring board document
because it; it’s not headlines. It’s not even themes yet. It just
prepares us. We’ve connected our product to our market and now
it prepares us to start thinking about what the specific themes for
the promotion pieces we’re going to do are.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And when we do that kind of thing basically there are four or five
different kinds of themes that that are; that kind of jump out it
because they’re proven to work over time. The first one is topical
and we’ve talked about that in the past. I’m going to kind of stick
with this model of a health newsletter promotion.
Bob Bly: 	Sure.
Clayton Makepeace: 	But so on a topical theme, you would; what you do is you go to a
library or you go to a Web site where you can access an archive of
Time Magazine, Newsweek magazine, the pop; the pop mag,
magazine and all and also look at magazines like Prevention and
other health oriented magazines. And you go back over the last six
months to a year and you’re just kind of scanning covers and
you’re looking for recurring themes that are topical. And it could
be about; recently for example in the health industry there’s been a
spade of research on the; the half of all heart attack victims who

-- 6 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 7 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 37
don’t have clogged arteries. They; they; four; over 400,000 a year
die with healthy arteries. Well why is that?
Harvard University and an institution in London came up with a;
the answer and it was published in the Journal of American
Medical Association and the answer is arterial inflammation that
can be caused by something as innocent as a gum infection. And
it’s cause a small piece of plaque to break loose and then triggers a
heart attack or a stroke and, and so there’s been a lot of reporting
on that. That means it’s in the forefront of our prospects’ minds
and it means that they have feelings about that. Okay? And the
feeling is holy cow, how do I make sure that I don’t have
inflammation in my arteries and fortunately there are answers for
that.
In the financial area it’s the easiest thing in the world because
there’s so much news and, and, and so many large, long term
trends on the; in the economy and also in the investment markets
that it’s fairly easy to identify them and you don’t necessarily even
have to go online and look at all of these, you know see what
people are writing about them. We know that oil prices are five
times higher than they were just a few years ago. We know that
gasoline prices are probably 50 percent higher than they were just
a short time ago. And we know that interest rates are going up
and, and this has been a long term trend and is likely to continue.
And we know that inflation is increasing and that that’s likely to be
a long term trend due to these gigantic government deficits.
We know that China’s coming on strong with a GDP growth of; in
double digits now; almost three times ours. We know India’s
coming on strong. That’s obvious ________ for; for a major
theme. So those are all topical and, and again topicality sells
because your prospect already knows about the trend. He already
has feelings about it.
In the financial area, it’s a sense of urgency. This thing is
happening. Is it too late for me to get in? How do I profit from it
and he; he just has a sense of urgency. He wants; he wants
guidance. So a topical theme is, is always a great way to go.
Bob Bly: 	Let me ask you a question. In health but especially in finance, can
you make the; the; can you conversely conclude that if copy is
pretty devoid of topicality and sometimes you see that where it
seems like it could have been written now as easily as 10 years
ago. That would be; would that be a negative because the prospect
would think hey, these people do not know what’s going on?

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 8 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 37
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely. Fortunately topicality isn’t the only theme you can
use. So if you don’t have; there are two traps here. Okay, the first
trap is you pick something that’s in the news and you believe will
be in the news for a year or more because you want your package
to work for a year or more. Trap No. 1 is the trend reverses.
Okay?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And you; you might have a kick butt control that’s mailing big
numbers but then somebody comes out with a piece of research
that says oh, never mind. Inflammation does not cause heart
attacks or somebody or; or the president suddenly cuts the budget
and balances it, inflation drops to zero, interest rates fall and
you’re out there in a; in; in the mail with a promotion on; about
rising interest rates being locked into the system. So that’s trap
No. 1. And, and it just illustrates why choosing your theme if
you’re going a topical route; route, it’s very important to select
something that you have a 90 or 99 percent chance that it will
continue for the foreseeable future.
The other trap with this is picking a theme that is over used in the
mail or that, as Bob says, is not particularly new news and maybe
it’s along term trend but it’s not at the top of the headlines and
people are not necessarily thinking about it or your lead comes off
as just kind of feeling stale. If you can’t come up with a hot
topical theme that you feel will be in the news or continuing for
some period of time then there are some other ways you can go.
Bob Bly: 	So what would be some other themes I could use?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well I was just talking about the spring door; board document that
I did.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Where we talked about what our; what our prospect is thinking and
feeling relative to the subject area we’ve selected for our doctor to;
to specialize in for a health newsletter. So, another way to go
would be what I call a prospect driven piece and it’s essentially a
lead where you connect with the strongest emotion that you’ve
been able to identify in the prospect relative to the general tablo
we’ve created. And the example I used earlier was on this thing
about aging without growing old.

Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And so, the idea there is to say okay, we’ve identified all of the

have about this. And you know we; we looked at how, you know,
how the prospect feels about sex relative to growing older. How
the prospect feels about physical performance; his; his or her
performance, ability to work and play as they grow older. We
talked about their own self; their own sense of self value. What are
the things that they draw value from in their life?
Let me figure a better way to say that. Each of us thinks of
ourselves as having value for very specific reasons and for men, it
may ____ -

Bob Bly: 	You mean because I’m very good looking for example.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s; yeah, drop dead gorgeous.
Bob Bly: 	Yes.
Clayton Makepeace: 	You’re a snappy dresser. Yeah. And so, you, Bob oh course it’s
obvious where he draws his value from. And but, but men tend to
draw our value from the fact that we’re; we’re wage earners, good
providers; from the fact that we; good competitors, physical
competitors that you know, we could kick anyone’s butt in the bar
and, and we’re more athletic maybe. Or we and, and many of us
draw value from that even when we’re old and fat. That doesn’t
tend to change.
We still see ourselves as 18 years old. We; women tend to draw
their; their sense of value from; from okay, I’m going to give some
stereotypes ______ -
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	You’re going to get in trouble but go ahead.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. But; but remember we are in the business of stereotypes.
When we select a mailing list, we are saying these prospects are
stereotypically this age group, this sex, this income and so forth.
And so in our business we recognize those stereotypes by the very
nature are never 100 percent accurate but there’s enough truth in
them that we can; we can mind them in some cases when we’re

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 10 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 37
convinced that they’re; there’s some truth in the stereotype. But
many of the women in our audience draw a lot of self worth from
the fact that they are nurturers and, and they; they feel that they
have tremendous value because they take care of their; their; their
spouse or they take care of their children or grandchildren or they
bake them cookies. They may not be totally taking care of them
but they’re doing things for them that are kind of nurturing and if a
woman gets ill, if she gets older, she’s unable to do that and a
major part of her value as a woman is stolen from her.
And she’s going to have very strong feelings about that. And so
the prospect driven lead will essentially identify the; the dominant,
resident emotion that you feel is strongest among your prospects.
We’ll call that person out and put their feelings into words in a
way that will have them saying this is for me and get them nodding
their head right away and then and then offer a solution. That;
that’s the prospect driven lead. You’re not talking about the
product.
You’re not talking about the value of what you’re going to give
them free right up front necessarily. You’re not talking about
benefits off the top. They come in the; in the deck copy. You still
need to get them on Page 1 but it’s not the main lead. Instead
you’re going to a prospect and you’re giving him a; a slug in the
gut by talking to him about something that he feels as deep as,
most powerful, most intermit, most compelling fears, frustrations
and desires.
Bob Bly: 	Do you remember John Francis Tse at all?
Clayton Makepeace: 	No.
Bob Bly: 	The copywriter. So he had written a famous package and it was
actually; I can’t remember the publication but I remember the
headline was and this is 20 years ago. I don’t think it would work
today but it was the same thing. It was Are You Sick and Tired of
Feeling Sick and Tired?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. Yep. Absolutely. That; that is a perfect example of the
prospect driven emotion or _____ emotion headline. My goodness
time is going let’s; let’s move on.
The next one is the one that every AWAI student knows by heart
and that’s the benefit driven headline and that’s just simply where
you present; present your benefit in the headline. There’s nothing
wrong with that. I found that its second best for me be; because as

we discussed before people; benefits tend to connect with your
intellect. They tend to rationalize, give you a reason why you
should buy a product and peop; 99 percent of our purchase
decisions are not made on a rational basis but; but instead on an
emotional basis and to fill an emotional need. Therefore the bene;
I personally, my opinion is that the benefit driven headline tends to
be weaker than the prospect driven lead.
However again, on my lead which I define as my first one to three
pages but is; but especially on Page 1 I; I make sure I include
benefits. But the benefits that I include tend to have more to do
with a sell; a reason why you should read this than they do about
my product or my _____________________.
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	You’re selling the promotion not the product.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. So the benefits I use on my cover tend to have to do
with, you know if you read this, you know, I’ll; I’ll make sure you
don’t have a heart attack, a stroke or; or I’ll, you know, I’ll show
you how to make a million dollars investing in rising oil prices.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The; the; the; the benefit is there. The next one is intrigue driven.
This is, to me, actually stronger than a benefit driven and, and
especially in the financial markets. A few years ago I did a lead
that was The Greatest Medical Breakthroughs since the Discovery
of Penicillin.
Bob Bly: 	And that’s a perfect example. John Tse, this guy I mentioned, he
wrote a package for Psychology Today which was a very
successful control and it was; I don’t know if you remember this.
Do You Close the Bathroom Door Even When You’re the Only One
Home?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. (Laughter) I have it. I love it. And it’s just intrigue.
Depending on how well you connect with where your prospect is
intrigue; intrigue driven can be very strong.
In the financial markets especially, Bill Bonner is famous for
calling someone else, it says and somebody at his company and
saying, “I got a great idea for a package. The headline is Man Who
Bought Microsoft at $5.00 a Share Reveals the Stock He’s Buying
Now”. You know? Well you know at the time before the stocks

split Microsoft was over $100.00 a share. They, obviously this guy

Now Bill didn’t have an answer. He just had a great headline idea
and he said, “It’s you guys problem to go find, you know, that guy
and find the answer but this is a great promotion idea”. And I; I
forget who it was; it could have been either John Ford or Michael

grand slam home run. But anyway, that’s the; kind of the indrigue;
intrigue driven thing. Works great in health, works great in
financial and so long as you know what it is your prospects want.
I told a client the other day look, you know, just tell me what your
prospect fears, is frustrated with or desires and I’ll give you a 4
percent control. To the degree that; that you’re accurate; if you’re
100 percent accurate, we’ll have a 4 percent control. If you’re 50
percent accurate, we’ll probably get a 2 percent control. But you
give me that information and then I can write an intrigue driven
headline that will absolutely call those guys out on those; on those
feelings. Or I’ll write a prospect driven benefit; prospect driven
dominant emotion headline that will; that will call them out.
Bob Bly: 	Or you can do a topical.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Or you could do a topical on that.
Bob Bly: 	And then benefit driven is not your favorite choice. What else is
there?
Clayton Makepeace: 	The other way and I don’t see this very often and it’s; it’s an
opportunity for; that I’m going to fully exploit. And that is all of
our clients have testimonials about their products and they have the
names and addresses of people who has sent those testimonials.
Why not have a testimonial driven lead? Why not find the
strongest testimonial; two or three strongest testimonials that; that
your; your client has and sit down and, and call those people and
talk to them on the phone and ask them how they felt when before
their problem was solved? Before they’d met the client and before
they bought the product, how did the problem; this; this product
solve; reduce the quality of their life?
Bob Bly: 	Can I give you a great example of that? You know Peggy –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure.

doing this. And you’ve got a very strong then testimonial driven

not as commonly used as other approaches or a story.

Bob Bly: 	Then the question come; arises. So you’ve given us choices of

theme. There’s the prospect driven theme but based on their
dominant emotion of feeling. There’s the benefit driven theme
which usually derives from the product. You know it stops
hemorrhoids or whatever.
There’s the intrigue driven; why close the bathroom door when

That; you can select from those themes but how do you select the
format for your direct mail promotion? Do you do that? Does the
client do that? How does that work?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Typically I’ll do it and essentially when choosing format you; you
have, you know, you can go with an envelope package or a
component package that’ll have, you know, a sales letter, a list
letter, a response device, possibly a flyer in it. You can go with a
digest form; format package which is typically 81/2 x 11. It’s also
called a booklog. 81/2 x 11 page size and they can run 48, 64
pages long, typically with a four color cover and two color insides.
You can go with a special report format which is 81/2 x 11 toned
down, very serious looking, very credible looking. You know not
with all the, you know, the guys jumping on the beach on the cover
and then all of the stock photos we’ve seen a million times in; in
magalogs.
You can go with magalogs or you can go with tabloids. Magalogs
look more like magazines. Tabloids, I call them the biggest,

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 15 of 37

saddest, happiest floozy in the biker bar. They; they are; they are
huge. They’re colorful. There’s eye candy everywhere you look.
Essentially you have; you have two decisions to make. The first
one is who am I talking to. If you’re talking to very serious
people; investors, people who are ill or if you’re addressing a
subject that people are very serious about; it could be something as
trivial as stamp collecting but the people that are into it are; I mean
this is serious to them. This is their life. They love it. Then you
are going to want a format that sends the message that we’re
serious too.
If on the other hand you have a; a product that appeals to a more
consumer marketplace, like for example a very general health
publication that has to do with; with avoiding disease that’s going
to a very broad market then a more consumerish look is a; is a
great way to go. And that would be more like a magalog or the
tabloid. With board room, tabloids work extremely well because
they’re going to a very broad marketplace. Their prospect is
anybody who has a; a checking account on, on their financial side.
On their health side, it’s anyone who has a body.

never to eat on an airplane. You know bill ______________ -

on just a second.
Bob Bly: 	Sure.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And then the message; just keep in mind though that the tone of
the message is reflected or either strengthened or weakened by the
format that you choose. And then again, let’s talk about just a
couple of other considerations here. If you’re going up against a
strong control, a good rule of thumb is to do exactly the same
format as the control.

-- 15 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 16 of 37

Bob Bly: 	That way if you should unfortunately you know it’s not that you
picked the wrong format.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well yeah. The; the way I look at it is not so much I’m worried
about losing. I’m more concerned that if; if I write a stronger copy
which oh course is my; my intension than the control, I don’t want
to lose because my package costs –
Bob Bly: 	More expensive.
Clayton Makepeace: 	More money. And if I’m writing against a strong control, I don’t
want to lose because the format I chose limited me to less copy
space than the control.
Bob Bly: 	Got it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So it’s very important to me in 99 percent of all; all cases to look at
the control. If it’s; if it’s a weird looking 20 page tabloid with you
know real floppy, not very thick, the client’s going to get a 20; a
weird looking 20 page tabloid from me. Once I have the control
and this just happened last week; once I have the control, I will
immediately go to the client and say okay, well I hated that format
in the first place but since we have a control I want to spin off a
digest version of it or a book, you know, a booklog or; or a special
report version. I have a very strong control out there for a net; for
a supplemental product and I suggested to the client several
months ago that we, as soon as we established it as a control that
we immediately spin off a digest format. And we’re; we’re around
our doubling date right now and the; and the digest is out pulling
the 24 page tabloid by about 50 percent.
And the; the control isn’t old enough to begin tiring yet so that’s a
big lift in response for him. Okay? But on my first outing, I went
up against his control which was a tabloid and I gave him exactly
the same number of pages so my fees cost exactly the same as the
control. So cost is a very important factor and you know consider
that seriously. 	This format question used to be the hardest thing
for me when I first started writing a pack. As soon as I sat down
and, and really thought about the numbers I’ve never had to worry
about what ____ format I –

and for the reasons you just stated that the most sensible thing is; is

-- 16 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 17 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 37
to; to, you know to duplicate or, or, or use the same format but
write a stronger copy.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. I’d like to just say one other thing. If; if the control is
an envelope package containing several components and especially
if the envelope is oversized, it’s important for copywriters to
understand the jumbo envelopes and other; other odd shaped
envelopes are custom jobs. That limits the number of times your;
your client can go into the mail with it because there’s quite often a
six to eight week fabrication period required for these odd shape
envelopes. So if you go with that format, you’re basically limiting
the number of mailings your client can do of your new control over
the next year. That’s not smart.
It’s; so the; the; my one caveat would be if the control is a
component package, that’s the one time that I would consider
going; breaking that rule and, and going with the self mailer
instead. They’re much quicker to produce and the client can
produce more mailings per year and make you greater royalties as
a result.
Bob Bly: 	Smart advice. I want to keep you on track so the next topic I want
to ask you about, you’ve written this package. Let’s say it’s a
tabloid or a magalog. Now you’re looking at your copy. How do
you know; how do you do the due diligence to look at your
headline and say yeah, that’s the best it could be? How do you rate
your headline?
Clayton Makepeace: 	You know I; I’ve been thinking about just doing an article on this
and, and or even putting up a Web page so that everybody could
just simply go in and, and click a button and give every headline
they write a rating of 1 to 5 on the basis of 11 questions.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And, and then get a grade for how strong the headline is. But
there; there are several things that I look for in a headline and No.
1 is; is pretty elementary and that’s _______. The purp; the No. 1
purpose of a headline is to get attention and I; I’ve often said that
the best headline in the world is sneaking up behind somebody
with a cap gun and pulling the trigger. You have their attention. A
headline doesn’t have to present every benefit in the world.
It doesn’t have to; you know, do anything really but grab attention.
You can do all of the other things that you need to do in your; in
your eyebrow or pre-head or in your deck copy and oh course, in

-- 17 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 18 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 37
the opening copy of; of the actual piece. So size; in; envision your
prospect at the mailbox, you’ve just opened the thing up especially
this time of year what do you got? He’s got 27 catalogs. Every
single one of them is 81/2 x 11 format.
He’s got his bills. He’s got letters from friends and relatives. He’s
got all of this other stuff in the mailbox and somewhere in that
stack of mail is your piece that your entire future is hanging on.
Okay? No. 1, size matters in terms of the headline.
Your piece will not be read if you don’t get his attention. You
won’t get his attention unless you catch his eye. So go with
bigness. No. 2 is resonance. How well does this headline resonate
with my reader?
Bob Bly: 	Example, do you make these mistakes in English?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. Yes. I; I; I think the resonance factor, if you do the other
things we’ve talked about in terms of connecting, identifying what
he’s thinking about himself relative to this subject and how he then
feels about those things; I think you will resonate with him. I think
he’ll see this and, and say this is for me. That’s; resonance is
basically calling out your prospect. Letting him know this is for
him.
Second thing is a benefit either implicit or explicit and again you
know; how strong is the benefit you’re offering? It doesn’t matter;
oh course as I said, I prefer on my cover to provide the benefits for
reading this because I’m asking this guy look, turn off the TV, tell
your wife to leave you alone for five minutes, tell the kids to leave
you alone for five minutes so you can read this because this is the
most important thing in your mailbox today and here’s why. You
know? So you know, I; I want his full attention and the way to do
that is to sell him on reading this. And so the benefits of reading
this piece to me are the key on the outside. _______ -
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Sell the promotion first not the product.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. If you’re writing shorter copy oh course, great, go straight

Clayton Makepeace: 	Does; ___ -
Bob Bly: 	I do have to interrupt. We do have someone who wants to know;
the question is very simple. What do you mean by a deck? He
doesn’t know that term.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh. Okay. Deck is the enlarged copy below the headline but
before the actual text cop; the running text begins in your
promotion.
Bob Bly: 	Some people might call it a subhead.
Clayton Makepeace: 	It; yeah. To me a subhead is more, you know, a small headline
breaking up columns of text.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	But this deck copy is; is not, you know, it’s smaller than the
headline. It’s bigger than the running text and –
Bob Bly: 	And it’s in between them.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. And it tends to expand upon the headline and then present
the benefits for reading this or the benefits of the product. After
you’ve done that and you, you can still do this in the deck or you
can actually begin moving into your text with it. Does it present a
horrifying alternative? Now both the benefits and the horrifying
alternative in this deck copy; the benefits should be explicit. The
horrifying alternative could be implicit or explicit.
You might actually say, I mean, Jim Russ once did a headline that
said Read This or Die. I mean that; that; that presents you with
two very clear choices. (Laughter)
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And the horrifying alternative is right there in the headline. But
this contrast, you know, between hot and cold, between benefit,
you know, where you’ll go if you read this and where you’ll stay
or go if you don’t read this, that contrast is extremely power. So
does your lead include a horrifying alternative? If you have a
solution for arthritis then you know, what is the alternative to that
solution? You know being crippled for the rest of your life, you
know?

-- 19 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 20 of 37

So you know think about the; about whether or not you’ve
adequately presented, you know, both sides of the coin. Now in
the other hand, some packages that you’ll do will be fear based
packages and so they’ll lead with the fear; the horrifying
alternative and then in that case that’s fine but follow it up with the
benefit copy; the reasons why you need to read this.
Bob Bly: 	Got it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The next one is does; is my lead emotionally driven? Does it
connect with how my prospect feels? Does it expand on those
feelings? Does; does; does it fully engage him emotionally? A lot
of writers don’t do that. A lot of writers, especially younger
writers who have been taught to focus on benefits, will go into the
text and just flatly state the benefits and move through the copy
and it comes across as kind of dry.
And if your subject matter is, you know, if you’re talking about
heart disease and you’re talking as if it were; as if this was some
kind of a Ph. D. treatise or something then you know, it doesn’t
work because; because it sounds like the; the speaker; the person
who’s; who’s writing these words is a cold fish. And, and it just
doesn’t work on that level. I, it can also undermine credibility. So
make sure that the; the; the; you haven’t infused this early copy
with the emotion and that it’s appropriate for the subject at hand.
The next one we talked about is a topical. Now again the; the; all
of the; the themes that we chose earlier that we talked about
earlier, you can tell; you can see it’s not a Chinese menu where
it’s; it’s, you know, you have to pick one.
This is a mix and match thing. You can; you can have something
that’s topical and emotional and benefit driven and I think the
strongest leads combine all of this. So if; if you’ve chosen a theme
that isn’t or a headline that isn’t particularly topical, look for ways
to bring in a topical element into the deck, into the opening copy.
And even if it’s; let’s say that you know; you’re talking about, you
know, bankruptcy, right? And so you’re; you’re appealing directly
to the prospect’s fear of bankruptcy or his desire to get out of debt,
working in; early into the copy some news story from Newsweek or
Time or Wall Street Journal about the fact that bank; that 1.5
million Americans will g bankrupt this year, maybe even showing
it. You know reproducing the article brings in this topical feel that
this is breaking news.
This is something I need to know. Specificity is the next question.
Do I have enough specifics in my headline and lead structure to

-- 20 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 21 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 21 of 37
make this credible? It’s one thing to say that the person writing to
you is the smartest person who ever lived on this subject in the
history of the Universe since the big _______ period.
That is a claim. It’s not a; it’s not necessarily a proven fact. But if
after having said that, you then demonstrate that this guy knows his
stuff by including specifics about the subject at hand. Maybe it’s
the number of people who have a heart attack with healthy arteries
this year. Maybe its how many medical studies have proven this
supplement to be safe and effective. Maybe it’s the name of the
doctor who originally discovered _________ back in 1940
whatever or 50 whatever or ADTA in ’46 or ’45 I think and you
know but; but have the specifics.
We had a; a lead for one of our packages that I did for Wise where
I; I was able to say 346 of the S&P500 companies are fudging their
books. You know? And it was; it was, you know, people looked
at that and they said man, this guy knows, you know, it’s not 350.
It was an odd number. Okay?
So it was specific. I see people all the time using headlines like
you know, Grow 10,000 Percent Richer or How I Meet; How I
Made So and So Grow, you know, 500 Percent Richer. Well
nobody grows 500 percent richer. They grow 473.2 percent richer.
So being more specific with the numbers and demonstrating your
knowledge of the subject in the lead copy brings enormous
credibility.
So rate your headline on that. Again we’ve talked about the
readership sell. You’ve got to ask yourself have I adequately sold
the prospect on reading what follows. Have I grabbed him by the
eyeballs, forced him down into the text and made it, you know, it’s
like; it; it’s like looking at a train wreck in slow motion only on the
good side. So it impossible for him to look away at this point and
if not, what can I do to strengthen that desire to read on? You
know the old thing about cliff hangers in the movies?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	You watch a 15 minute serial on one Saturday and it left you so
excited about seeing what happens next you would wait a whole
week and come back to the theatre on the next Saturday to see
what; whether the guy fell off a cliff or not. The; the readership
sell is absolutely essential. Then is there an element of irony or
intrigue? People love this whole idea that; that something that

-- 21 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 22 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 37
helps you in this way hurts you in that way or something that
you’ve been told about a particular subject is now being proven
wrong or even if it’s right, it also causes a problem in this other
area. Any irony you can bring to the discussion and this tends to
be usually in deck copy or opening copy; sometimes in the
headlines but this idea of irony is particularly powerful.
And it’s not, it’s; it’s a real key to me to; to a successful headline
or lead and it’s not always easy to do. It’s not always possible to
do. But when you hit on some fact that you’re going to reveal that
the reader will say; it’ll be a real head scratcher, real ironic or; or
on the, you know, it could be just intriguing then you’ve got
something. But the irony thing, I know Peter Bolosky who was an
in-house guy for Phillips for years and I don’t think he was even
writing copy at first for them. I think he was in marketing. He
eventually talked them into letting him write a promotion piece and
it did very well and he ended up going out on his own freelance.
And he’s very good at this whole thing of finding ironies related to
the subject at hand and it just; it hooks you in. It just works every
single time for me. And then the final thing is does my headline
index structure present a proposition? And there are whole books
written on this. If you; if you haven’t read the books that we’ve
recommended particularly Ogilvy & Rosser Reeves, John Caples,
I urge you to do so.
And if you want a book list write to us and we’ll get one to you,
our recommended reading list. But there’s been a lot writ; written
on this whole idea of a proposition and the proposition can be in
one of two general categories that; that particularly work well.
One the; the classic proposition is a way of asking a question or
creating intrigue or irony in a prospect’s mind. If doctors treat sick
people all day long why don’t they get sick?
Bob Bly: 	Great example.
Clayton Makepeace: 	There’s; there’s a proposition. It; it’s not saying hey, I’m going to
sell you something. It’s just; it’s something that stops you cold
and say yeah, how come, you know? Another way of doing and
that’s particularly powerful. We could do an hour just on that. By
the way, if any of you do not read Bencivenga Bullets, do it.

Bob Bly: 	So why don’t we move forward. So that’s how to evaluate the

does nothing else but get attention it’s successful. But now you’ve

dear investor or dear reader? 	How do I write opening copy that
hooks them and doesn’t let them go?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well I want to move through these quickly. The first thing that I
do because we’ve covered some of this before.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The first thing with; now we’re talking about you’re actually in the
text. You’re in the beginning –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Of the sales; the; the running text of the sales copy.
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Your actual salutation.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. The first thing is connect with the reader at the point
of his need. You talk to the reader about the benefits you’re going
to bring to his life and this paragraph needs to connect with
everything that went on before. So you’re following on the theme
but now you have an opportunity to expand on it even further. It’s
like the; the; the headline is the point of the nail that goes into the
wood. The deck copy is a wider spot in the nail and, and now
you’re just continuing to widen out, widen out.
So at this point now you’re connecting back with that but you’re
expanding. The first time to do that I like to do is to immediately
present in longer copy in the text the reasons why receiving this
particular communication is by far the most important thing that
has happened to my prospect, you know, since; since he was born.
You know this; I have; I need to get him engaged and to
understand that if he; if he reads what follows, these wonderful

-- 23 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 24 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 37
things are going to happen in his life. If he doesn’t, they won’t
happen and some terrible things might happen. The; and that’s
presenting the horrifying alternatives and this is your opportunity
to go into slightly longer copy about that.
Then next thing you do is you go back; now we’re repeating what
we did on the; on the cover or in our headline structure but we’re
doing it in a different order and we’re using longer copy to do it.
Now you establish your credibility. On a financial package, my
next step then is to say you know not; not only am I telling you
that you can do these things or that I will do these things for you,
not only am I telling you that if you don’t let me do these things,
these bad things are going to happen. There’s what I’ve done for
other people just like you in the last 12 months and in a financial
package, that’s where I put my track record. You know?
I recommended oil stocks 12 months before oil took off. We made
1,000 percent on our money. I recommended interest vehicles just
before interest rates started rising. We made 345 percent on our
money. You know I did these things, right and the more specific
the better and that’s your track record.
If you don’t have that kind of track record, like for example in a
supplement product you won’t have that kind of track record, this
is a place to insert five quick testimonials. You know? Again,
each one of these testimonials saying you know what, I didn’t
think it worked either but; but this worked for me and here’s what
it did. My blood pressure dropped 20 points. My; my blood sugar
dropped.
My, you know, cholesterol dropped. They cancelled my heart
surgery. You know those kinds of things. But it; they’re from real
people ____ testimonial. This is a proof element segment of the
running text.
Once you’ve done this, you don’t necessarily have to go back to it
if you do a good job on it. Although I do use sidebars through the
text to; to return to it but not in the running text. 	And then; then I
tend to; all of this remember is aimed at selling the reader on
reading the entire piece and all of this should be accomplished the
first couple of pages of text at the most. So I sell them on; I’m;
I’m selling them on reading what; that follows; what follows. So
at that point; at this point I’ll say spend a few minutes with me now
and here’s what’s going to happen bang, bang, bang; maybe three,
four, five things, bullets real quick. You know?

-- 24 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 25 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus

And then I follow and, and then I follow that immediately by
saying something like plus in a month; you know I’ll say
something like every fact and figure in this spell; special bulletin
today is taken from a volume that I’ve just; that’s hot off the press.
I’ve just completed that I want to send to you free because this
topic is so important and in a moment I’ll invite you and the title of
this; the; this volume is this. And in a moment I’ll invite you to
send it; send for it free. And then at that point you dive in. Now
it’s time to fulfill on all; everything that you’ve told them that you
would tell me.
So this is the; the; the main body, the middle of the; of the; of the
sales message is where you develop your theme. And if; if you’re
talking about the product, you can begin by talk; by talking about;
empathizing for example with how terrible it is to have this
problem. Go straight to introducing the product, explain all of the
things the product will do for the prod; for the prospect and, and
then move into your premium sale or your offer. If it’s an
advertorial kind of piece where you’re trying; you’re bringing
value to the prospect’s life in the piece not just selling a product
which; which I highly recommend for most promotions, you dive
into the reasons why, for example in a; in a financial package, why
this trend is going to continue. And you talk about or if it’s a
health package, you talk about why it’s a dead lock cinch that
particular substances will reverse your heart disease or do
whatever they’re going to do.
Bob Bly: 	Right. So those are some of the things that we should do right but
you’ve also promised us and I’m reading from the notes you sent
me which I’m curious about, the No. 1 blunder that even A list
writers make. So what’s the big mistake that people do that screws
all this up?
Clayton Makepeace: 	There’s three things. The first thing is falling in love with the
subject and I’m really guilty of this. A client comes to me and
says okay, I want you to write a package about EDT; EDTA
______ therapy and I don’t know what _____ it is. I’ve never
heard of it before. And so I; I start reading the research and as I
read it, you know, a great salesman always sells himself first and a
great salesman is always a great prospect.
And I’ve got the kitchen cabinets full of supplements to proof it.
But I start reading and I start falling in love with the subject and
next thing you know I’m going oh, that is so cool and I’ll write a
whole sidebar on something that’s really extraneous and, and kind
of takes it; just because it’s interesting. You know? And, and, and

-- 25 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 26 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 37
so it takes us down a path that we don’t need to go down and it;
it’s; it tends to be confusing. And I did this once with horrible
results with a 32 page tabloid where I absolutely fall in love with
the subject and it was impen; impenetrable.
We started out on one thing. We started developing the thing but
then all of the sudden I started hoping that my market would;
would find this as fascinating as I did and obviously, they didn’t.
It bombed big time. Second thing is forgetting the prospect. Once
you begin writing about the; if it’s an advertorial the major trend
that you’re hook; hitching your wagon to or if it’s, you know, a
health piece, you’re talking about the various substances that that
can address whatever health problem that you’re addressing.
People tend, younger writers especially tend to kind of forget about
the prospect. It’s like okay, we talked about you. Now we’re
going to talk about me. The point of all of this is all of it needs to
come back to the prospect and you know; it drives me crazy when
I see copy where it says, Vitamin; Vitamin D quails inflammation
and reduces risk to the heart.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That copy stinks. You know? What; what you need to be saying
is look buddy, you know, if you’re worried about dropping dead
instantly of a heart attack because you have an inflamed artery, you
need to know, you know, that Vitamin D is nature’s most powerful
informa; inflammation soother and. And that one piece of
information can save your life and I know that because it could
save 438,000 people a year who die with healthy arteries. The
difference in that is the stilted approach talking about the heart and
talking instead about your heart; you the prospect and not only
that. About how you don’t have, you know, you won’t have to
worry about this once you take care of making sure you have
enough Vitamin D.
Bob Bly: 	So the writer up front does what you say and bonds with the
prospect but now when it’s time to dive in; he has this scientific
research on ingredient one, ingredient two.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	And he’s just regurgitating that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. Absolutely. Another blunder that that writers make
and this is Dan Rosenthal’s big, you know, complaint and that is

-- 26 of 37 --

The Profit Center 	6_Bonus_37_Proven_Secrets 	Page 27 of 37
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus

clarity of vision. It kind of ties back to my thing of falling in love
with; with the subject but you need to create a feeling of
momentum in the package. You need to feel like you’re actually
going some place with each paragraph.
You don’t want to be circling back and remaking old points. You
need to be feeling like you’re moving ahead and you need to feel
like there isn’t; there aren’t any side tabs here. The sidebars are
not going to take you off in a different direction if you have
sidebars. They’re going to support what you’ve already said. And
they can support it with a chart or a figure or a quote but they
support what’s said in the main text. That; that clarity of vision
thing is very important and it’s important too when you get down
to the offer.
In the text for example when you’re; when you’re presenting the
offer, I only present one offer and I only present my best one.
When they get to the response device –
Bob Bly: 	Then there’s the choices.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Then you know if they want to cheap out they can get the one year
offer instead of the two year offer but it’s going to cost them more
per issue and they’re not going to get as many free gifts. So why
even mention it in the running text, you know? So.
Bob Bly: 	So you’d mentioned that you need the offer and the sidebars to
support clarity of visions, to support your main theme. So what are
some of the sidebars that I should be including in a long piece to

Clayton Makepeace: 	Let me; let; I’ll just mention –
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Eleven of them real quickly and then we can move on. The first
kind of sidebar that I try to in; intro; include is a biography on the;
the person that’s the author of the piece. You know the –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The person that’s supposedly writing this, to demonstrate that he’s
qualified to have an opinion on this subject. And, and also that he
has a heart for the reader. He’s on a crusade. He has compassion
for them. He’s dedicated his life to solving this problem.

Second thing is track record and again, a financial package that is;
that’s you know, the trades we’ve made and how much money we
made. In a health package, it could just be testimonials. The next
one is little readership and readership sells throughout and that
would be a pull quote from the text. And quite often I’ll write the
pull quotes first. What; what could I say on this spread in a pull
quote that would make it absolutely impossible for the; the reader
not to, you know, dive into the; the small copy, you know and read
it thoroughly?
What could I say here? Usually it’s a benefit or it’s a threat. It’s
either; it’s either, you know, when you do this one thing, this will
happen. This good thing will happen. Or it could be; it could be if
you’re not getting enough Vitamin D, this bad thing will happen.
You know?
But and it usually includes a photograph of the author speaking the
words. Again that’s a readership sale. Again, you’re making the
same sale again. Credibility the next one is and again that can be
broken down in testimonials, media mentions about the author that
bring him credibility, appearances if he was on Nite Line or some
TV show or; or in some publication use that. If he; if he’s been
quoted in the Wall Street Journal show a picture of the Wall Street
Journal.
It’s one thing to say it, you know, he’s; he; Wall Street Journal
calls him all the time for comment. It’s another to show a picture
of the Wall Street Journal and next to that and say that, you know,
the; the; the; Mr. So and So’s insights and advice frequently appear
in the pages of the Wall Street Journal. And the other one is case
histories. We’ve talked about that before. That’s basically a
testimonial but written in a narrative.
So you’re almost living through this. It’s like watching an episode
of ER. You know you’re actually living through this; vicariously
this particular experience this person had. Process sidebars, this is
a; this is a biggy. Everyone who takes supplements does it on
faith. You know?
Show how the thing works, you know? Give me a visual image of
how this particular nutrient does the thing it’s supposed to do.
That can be very, very strong. And another one is premium ads.
These are simply half page to full page ads that demonstrate the
value of the free gift that you’re going to give away usually
bulleted copy but this is laid in the piece and it really helps
dimensionalize the value of the offer.

Clayton Makepeace: 	Exactly. Right. And then finally the guarantee. I; I use a
guarantee in three places. I use it in the running text after I’ve
presented my offer. 	I say it’s only; it’s usually this much but you
only pay this plus you get all these things free.
And then I have my guarantee copy on the same spread where I

out and certificate border so it looks all nice and official or you
know in a very personal sounding letter from the author or even
something that looks like a contract. And we’ll talk about that in a

Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s exactly right. That copy sucks. That it’s absolutely
horrible. First of all you’re raising the specter that they may not be
satisfied. Right? That’s a major blunder.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The guarantee copy should be written you must be absolutely
delighted, otherwise and then give them the option. Instead of
saying hey, you might think this sucks and if it does, we’ll give
you your money back. You know? So, so again remember the
guarantee you’re selling the product and you’re selling the new
relationship. It’s not simply a risk reliever.
A lot of writers sleep; sleepwalk through that. It’s a major
mistake. Secondly, you; it gives you an opportunity to recite your;
your benefits. This product must do this, must do that, must do the
other thing otherwise, all right then; now your guarantee is gaining
some power. Right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I; I; I also say you know, your; your; your subscription to XYZ
plus your free copy of this, your free copy of that, your free copy
of this, your free copy of that, you know? All of this stuff must do
this or otherwise just let me know and I’ll rush you a full refund.
The; the other thing on this thing is that when you’re writing the
guarantee rushing of refund is important. It’s not something like
you know just let me know, I’ll send you your money back. It’s
important to say you know; this is going to happen fast.
It’s important to give them a specific and say whatever you can
say; give them a performance specific. In the next 12 months I
will do these things for you and if it’s the financial product and,
and it’s not a regulated product. If it’s a newsletter advisory not an
actual security you’re selling, you can say you know what, I’m
going to make you a promise. In the next 12 months I will put an
absolute minimum of $5,000.00 50 times what you paid for my
newsletter into your brokerage account. If I can’t do that I; I won’t
keep your money. You just let me know and I’ll rush you a full
refund of; of; of every penny you paid.

you risk nothing.

maybe it’ll work better if I move this over here or if I did this a

don’t want the back end to be your copy.
We’re talking the last three or four pages of copy here in long
copy. You don’t want your back end to feel like there’s a
disconnect from the front end so I go back to my main theme. If
my main headline was give me 90 days and I’ll help you then I
have a subhead that says I’m; I’m standing by to make the next 90
days the best of your life. And then I’ll repeat the benefits that I
promised them when I; when I dimensionalized the free gifts they
got or the product itself. Then I’ll recite the value statements that I
gave when I justified the price of the product in the offer section.
Then I’ll dimen; fully dimensionalize that value by comparing it to
something of far less import that they’re paying more for now.

Clayton Makepeace: 	My old Star Bucks trick. Then I remind them that there’s no risk
and this is the part I was just talking about where you recite the
guarantee. Then I ask for the sale and when asking for the; and,
and you have to ask for the sale and you do it repeatedly in the
closing. A lot of young writers forget to do this but you have to
tell them now is the moment. We are; you are at a crossroads. We
can either go on together or you can go on alone and here are the
things that await you down that path.
But if you choose to go with me, here are the things that go; that
await you down this path and you draw that contrast. And, and
you; and tell them this is the moment. Then you have to tell them
specifically what to do. I want you to dial toll free 1-800 blah,
blah, blah right now or complete and, and return the enclosed order
form in the envelope provided. Or jump on the Internet and, and
log in such and such a place and sign up.

sale again and remind them of the guarantee and then repeat your
main theme; your main headline and deck theme. You know
which, you know, we; we discussed. Repeat that theme so it’s tied
in right here at the close and at this point, you’re; you’re; you’re
really finishing with a flourish. Make sure your closing line just
before the signature ties in with that theme as well.

come off sounding like a pitchman; it’s so much better if your
order form is a cert; is a free gift certificate or a contract, better
health contract.

Clayton Makepeace: 	Enrollment form. Just anything but order form. It; it’s; it’s too

of; of jet airplanes then an order form is fine. If you’re selling a

that it’s extremely powerful. Make sure you headline it.

reps were and how fast the product came; actual testimonials to
that effect. If it’s a financial newsletter or a health newsletter, I’ll

in people’s lives. Again credibility at the point of sale.

Clayton Makepeace: 	All right. Let’s; tell you what guys if –
Bob Bly: 	Now that’s the day before Thanksgiving. I don’t know if that’s
going to work for everybody.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh. Sorry.
Bob Bly: 	It’s fine with me but you know a lot of people go home early.
What do you want to do?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. I’ll tell you what. Why don’t we do it next Wednesday and
for those who can’t join us, we’ll make the MP3 available.
Bob Bly: 	So why don’t we say guys we’ll go from 12:00 p.m. to 1:00 p.m.
next Wednesday and you get a six call and it’ll be; it’ll be nothing
but wrap up and questions and answers. So if you sent me
questions, everyone who sent me an email today, we’re going to
get to it and if you didn’t, Julie give the address again.
Julie McManus: 	Feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com.
Bob Bly: 	So we’ll; we’ll meet at; we’ll meet at noon and we’ll go for as long
as it takes to get the questions. If it takes an hour, we’ll go to 1:00
p.m. If there’s three questions and we; and there’s more than that
but you know, we can answer them in 15 minutes that’s what we’ll
do but I; I think it’ll take an hour just based on what I have already.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And if you all are going to Thanksgiving dinner and –
Bob Bly: 	You can listen.
Clayton Makepeace: 	You can’t make the call, don’t worry about it we will make the
MP3 available to you but I; I just want to make sure we fulfill on
what we promised you so.
Bob Bly: 	Terrific idea.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Everyone have a great Thanksgiving.
Bob Bly: 	Yes. And we’ll see a lot of you next Wednesday.

-- 36 of 37 --
